fields.Identifying_Data_Chief_Complaint: 14-year-old brought by school counselor after scratching the words 'I hate me' into forearm with pencil during class.
fields.Subjective: Patient is a 14-year-old who scratched 'I hate me' into forearm with mechanical pencil during math class. Teacher and counselor intervened. Patient explains they hate themselves because they are 'failing at everything' - academics declining, recently rejected from school musical, feel like disappointment to parents. Reports this is first time self-harming but has thought about it before. Denies suicidal ideation, plan, or intent. Describes overwhelming feelings of inadequacy and self-loathing. Sleep and appetite normal. Has small friend group. Very close with younger sibling. Parents are high-achieving physicians with high expectations. Currently on no medications. Previously saw therapist at age 11 for anxiety but family discontinued. No prior self-harm. (School Counselor Patricia Martinez, MA)

Mental_Status_Examination.Appearance: Words visible scratched in forearm, neat otherwise
Mental_Status_Examination.Thought_Content: Denies SI; self-hatred; NSSI as expression of self-loathing

fields.Diagnostic_Impression: Major depressive disorder; non-suicidal self-injury; severe self-esteem issues related to perfectionism and parental pressure.
fields.Psychosocial_Context: Academic decline; rejected from musical; high-achieving physician parents; discontinued previous therapy; close with sibling; friend group; first self-harm incident.
Assessment_and_Plan: Low risk for suicide but high concern for depression and self-loathing.
Words scratched superficially; cleaned and bandaged.
Remove access to sharp objects including pencils when distressed.
Resume therapy addressing perfectionism, self-esteem, parental expectations.
Family therapy essential to address achievement pressure.
Help parents understand impact of expectations on patient's mental health.
Reframe rejection and academic struggles as normal, not failure.
Safety plan emphasizing talking to trusted adults when self-critical.
Follow-up with therapist within one week.
Safe for discharge with treatment engagement and family education.
fields.Author: Patricia Martinez, MA (School Counselor)
